title: Amazon Account Health: The Metrics That Keep You Selling
excerpt: One bad metric can suppress your listings or suspend your account. Here are the 2026 thresholds that matter and how to stay safely inside them.

blocks.text: Your Account Health Rating (AHR) is the single number Amazon uses to decide whether you keep selling. It rolls up customer-service, shipping, and policy-compliance metrics into a score, and crossing the wrong threshold can suppress listings, cost you the Buy Box, or deactivate your account — sometimes before any warning email arrives.

items.label: AHR score for healthy standing (scale 0–1000); new sellers start at 200
Order Defect Rate ceiling — aim for under 0.5%
Rolling window most customer metrics are measured over

blocks.text: Each metric has a hard line. Falling below standard on even one can trigger review across your whole account. These are the current US thresholds — verify them in your Account Health dashboard, as Amazon adjusts enforcement periodically.

blocks.caption: Key Amazon seller metrics and thresholds (verified May 2026 — confirm in Seller Central)

cells: Orders confirmed after the ship-by date
Deliveries arriving by the promised date

blocks.title: FBA removes three of these
blocks.text: If you use FBA, Amazon handles fulfillment and delivery — so the shipping metrics (late shipment, tracking, on-time delivery) come off your risk profile. One reason FBA sellers get smoother metrics automatically.
ODR is the one to guard most

Order Defect Rate is the heavyweight. It combines 1- and 2-star feedback, A-to-Z Guarantee claims, and chargebacks over a rolling 60-day window. Multiple problems on one order count as a single defect. The math is unforgiving at low volume: with 1,000 orders, just 11 defects puts you over 1%.

blocks.title: The cost hits before any warning
blocks.text: Sellers above 1% ODR are automatically disqualified from the Buy Box — and roughly 82% of Amazon sales go through it. You lose most of your sales before Amazon sends a single email. That's why prevention beats appeals.

items.title: Open the dashboard weekly, not monthly
items.text: A violation caught on Tuesday is far cheaper to fix than one Amazon flags on Friday. Check the Account Health page every week.

items.title: Reply to customers within 24 hours
items.text: Fast responses prevent complaints from escalating into A-to-Z claims and negative feedback — including weekends.

items.title: Watch policy compliance, not just service
items.text: Most sellers fixate on ODR and shipping, then get blindsided by an IP or restricted-product warning. These can hit hard.

items.title: Keep a buffer
items.text: Target metrics well below the limits (ODR under 0.5%, late shipment under 2%) so a single bad week doesn’t push you over.

blocks.text: If your account is already flagged, the next guide walks through building a Plan of Action to recover.
